stem: A free mobile game collects detailed data about every player: location every minute, full contact list, microphone usage, and a record of every other app installed.
model_answer: Ethical concerns: pupils may not realise the depth of data being collected; the data goes far beyond what's needed to play the game; selling on data harms the user even if the game itself works. Legal concerns: under UK GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018 the developer needs a lawful basis (usually informed consent), must minimise to what's necessary, and must allow deletion on request. Cultural concerns: shapes a 'free game = your data is the product' norm. A balanced answer notes the developer's commercial argument (free-to-play needs ad targeting), the user's argument (informed consent must be genuine, not buried in a 30-page T&C), and recommends a clear opt-in for each data class plus deletion on demand.
review_notes: Teacher-marked, level-marked. L1 = lists concerns. L2 = applies to game context. L3 = balances developer/user, names a specific law (GDPR / DPA 2018), and ends with a defensible recommendation.

parts.label: (a)*
parts.prompt: Discuss the ethical and legal issues raised by this level of data collection. Recommend changes the developer should make.

mark_points.text: ethical: scope of collection far exceeds what the game needs
ethical: users (especially young ones) may not understand what they consent to
legal: UK GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018 — lawful basis + data minimisation
legal: right to be informed and to request deletion
balances developer's commercial side (free-to-play needs revenue) against user rights
specific recommendation: per-class opt-in / clearer T&Cs / deletion on demand
